metadata.summary: Four-stage process confirming right requirements via quality scorecard, technique selection, issue log, and stakeholder sign-off before design.

content.title: Requirements Validation and Sign-Off
content.summary: A four-stage process that confirms requirements build the right thing (not that they are built right). A BA-led quality scorecard grades each requirement against eight attributes (correct, complete, unambiguous, consistent, testable, traceable, feasible, necessary), a technique is selected (walkthrough, inspection, prototype review, simulation), findings are captured in a structured issue log, and a human stakeholder provides an explicit sign-off before design begins.

why.p: Development built on unvalidated requirements produces software that is correct but wrong. Wiegers (Software Requirements, 3rd ed.) and BABOK v3 ch. 8 both cite requirements defects caught before development as 10-100x cheaper to fix than post-delivery rework. Agents amplify the risk by generating plausible but invented requirements; structured validation with human sign-off closes the loop on summarization drift.

list.item: Before flipping an SDD feature from todo/ to in-progress/ — validate that spec.md AC matches what the stakeholder asked, not what the elicitation agent inferred
After a non-trivial elicitation session to catch summarization drift
When a major scope change arrives mid-flight, before resuming /faion
Pre-baseline gate: locking a spec.md version before design starts
Prototype is available and users can perform task-based walkthroughs to expose gaps
Throwaway spikes or research tasks — use a stop condition and brief, not a validation session
Pre-elicitation: validating empty or aspirational requirements is theatre
Pure operational runbook tweaks (cron edits, nginx vhost) — smoke tests, not validation
Strictly internal refactors with no behavior change
Post-launch: use Solution Evaluation and feedback loops instead

rule.statement: Apply validation before development (are we building the right thing?) and verification after implementation (are we building it right?); never conflate the two gates.
rule.rationale: Swapping these gates produces verified implementations of wrong requirements — expensive to fix post-delivery. BABOK v3 ch. 8 and ISO/IEC/IEEE 29148:2018 both mandate this sequencing.

rule.statement: Grade every requirement against all eight attributes before declaring it ready for design; a requirement that fails any attribute cannot be baselined.
rule.rationale: Skipping even one attribute creates known defects that surface during design or testing; the cost of fixing a baselined defect is 10-100x the cost of fixing it during validation (Wiegers, Software Requirements, 3rd ed.).

list.item: Correct — accurately represents stakeholder needs (verify against elicitation source, not the spec itself)
Complete — all necessary information is present (no TBD, no placeholder text)
Unambiguous — only one interpretation is possible (test: ask two stakeholders to paraphrase independently)
Consistent — no conflicts with other requirements in the same document or related documents
Testable — a test can be written to verify completion (if you cannot write a Given/When/Then, the requirement fails)
Traceable — links to its elicitation source and its downstream test case
Feasible — can be implemented within known constraints (technology, budget, time)
Necessary — delivers business value without gold-plating (nothing beyond the stakeholder's stated need)

rule.statement: Select validation technique based on requirement type: walkthrough for early-stage textual requirements, inspection for critical-system requirements, prototype review for UI/UX requirements, simulation for process requirements.
rule.rationale: A text walkthrough cannot validate a user interface; a formal inspection is overkill for low-criticality narrative requirements. Matching technique to requirement type maximizes detection at minimum cost.

list.item: Walkthrough — informal, author-led, collaborative; best for early-stage, pre-baseline requirements
Inspection — formal, checklist-driven, defect-focused; best for safety-critical, regulated, or high-risk requirements
Prototype review — visual and task-based; best for UI/UX requirements where users perform observable tasks
Simulation — step-through of process scenarios; best for BPMN-modeled process requirements
User acceptance review — business stakeholder-led; best for final business validation before baseline

section.name: session-preparation

rule.statement: Cap any single validation session at 25 requirements; schedule multiple sessions for larger requirement sets rather than extending session length.
rule.rationale: Stakeholder attention drops sharply after 90 minutes; 200 requirements in one session produces rubber-stamp approvals that miss critical defects. Segmentation by functional area maintains focus and accountability.

rule.statement: Name specific individuals for each reviewer role before scheduling; "Product Owner TBD" is not an acceptable agenda entry.
rule.rationale: Sessions without the actual decision-maker are preparation exercises, not validation. Named accountability prevents proxy approvals that lack authority.

section.reference: Validation session agenda with time slots and participant roles

section.name: issue-log-and-sign-off

rule.statement: Classify every issue with severity (H=blocks design, M=needs clarification before implementation, L=wording) and root-cause class (missing info, ambiguous wording, conflict, infeasible, gold-plating) before the session ends.
rule.rationale: Without severity discipline, all issues look equal and re-validation scope cannot be determined; without root-cause class, fix strategies are guesswork.

rule.statement: Require a named human to provide explicit per-AC approve|defer|reject verdict in the sign-off form; bulk approval of a document section is not acceptable.
rule.rationale: Bulk sign-offs are the canonical rubber-stamp failure mode; per-AC verdicts force reviewers to engage with individual requirements and create an auditable record.

section.reference: Sign-off form with per-AC verdict fields and conditions tracking

antipattern.description: Stakeholder signs off without having read the requirements document.
antipattern.reason: Rubber-stamp approval creates a false baseline; defects discovered during design or testing are traced back to an approved requirement, making them expensive change requests rather than cheap pre-baseline fixes.

rule.statement: Run validation as four sequential stages: (1) quality scorecard, (2) session preparation, (3) human-led session with agent transcription, (4) resolution gate before baseline.
rule.rationale: A single-pass validation prompt conflates analysis, planning, decision-making, and recording — tasks with different authority levels. Staged handoffs enforce the separation between agent preparation and human decision-making.

list.item: Stage 1 — Quality Scorecard: a research subagent loads spec.md, design.md, elicitation notes, and emits a per-requirement grade against all eight attributes with evidence_quote from the source (not the spec itself)
Stage 2 — Session Preparation: an agent selects technique, drafts agenda, assigns named reviewers, and distributes pre-read packet
Stage 3 — Human-led session: agent takes minutes, transcribes decisions, writes structured issue log with severity and root-cause class. No agent decisions.
Stage 4 — Resolution gate: agent verifies every issue is resolved or explicitly deferred before allowing baseline; /faion blocks done/ move if open issues remain

example.title: Quality scorecard prompt
example.note: Enforces source-evidence discipline and rejects invented requirement IDs.
example.code: <role>BA validator. Score each requirement against 8 quality attributes.</role> <inputs> <spec>{spec_md}</spec> <design>{design_md}</design> <source>{elicitation_notes}</source> </inputs> <rules> Output JSON array. Per item: {req_id, correct, complete, unambiguous, consistent, testable, traceable, feasible, necessary, severity, evidence_quote, recommendation} - Each attribute: pass|fail|na. severity: high|medium|low. - evidence_quote: exact substring from spec/design/source justifying the call. - No prose outside JSON. No invented req_ids. </rules>

example.title: Session minutes prompt
example.code: <task>From transcript {transcript_url}, emit a JSON issue log: {ac_id, issue, severity, root_cause, owner, resolution, resolution_status}. resolution_status in: open|deferred|resolved. Refuse to set resolved without an evidence_quote from the transcript.</task>

antipattern.description: Agent generates sign-off form with "Approved by: Product Owner" when no human has approved.
antipattern.reason: Hallucinated approvals create false baselines; any resolved/approved state must link to a verifiable evidence anchor (chat permalink, ticket transition, signed PDF hash).

antipattern.description: Agent asked "are these requirements OK?" trends toward yes (confirmation-bias scoring).
antipattern.reason: LLMs are politeness-biased; use adversarial framing — "find at least 3 ways each requirement could be misinterpreted" — before asking it to grade.

antipattern.description: Agent quotes spec.md back to itself as evidence_quote instead of citing the elicitation source.
antipattern.reason: Self-referential evidence cannot detect requirements that accurately restate an incorrect elicitation; evidence must come from the source document under review, not the artifact being validated.

antipattern.description: Agent proposes new requirements during the validation session ("while we're here, we should also...").
antipattern.reason: Validation modifies existing requirements; it does not extend scope. Extensions return to elicitation so they can be properly captured, analyzed, and prioritized before being baselined.

antipattern.description: Agent runs tests and labels the result "validation".
antipattern.reason: Validation = are we building the right thing (pre-development); verification = are we building it right (post-implementation). These are separate gates with different artifacts and authorities.

rule.statement: Enforce three mandatory human checkpoints: reviewer selection, decision capture during the session, and sign-off. Agents prepare and transcribe; humans decide.
rule.rationale: An agent does not have authority to sign off requirements; the signature must be from a named human with documented decision authority, auditable against an org chart or RACI.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Run validation as a 4-stage chain inside the SDD lifecycle. Stage 1: a research subagent loads spec.md, design.md, source elicitation notes, and any related data-analysis.md / interface-analysis.md, then emits a per-requirement quality scorecard against the 8 attributes (correct, complete, unambiguous, consistent, testable, traceable, feasible, necessary). Stage 2: a session-preparation agent picks the technique (walkthrough vs. inspection vs. prototype review vs. simulation) based on requirement type, drafts agenda + pre-read packet, and assigns reviewers. Stage 3 is human-led — agent only takes minutes, transcribes decisions, and writes a structured issue log. Stage 4: a re-validation gate verifies that every issue is resolved or explicitly deferred before allowing baseline. /faion should refuse to advance any task whose source AC has unresolved validation issues.

list.item: faion-sdd-executor-agent — drives the per-task validation gate; reads issue log, blocks done/ move if open issues remain
/faion — wires validation as a quality gate between todo/ → in-progress/ (initial validation) and in-progress/ → done/ (final sign-off proxy)

brainstorm — diverge phase: enumerate edge cases the requirements may miss (security, locale, error, concurrency); converge phase: keep only items reviewers care about. Use before the human session, not instead of it.
improver — periodic audit: scans spec.md files in done/ for AC that drifted from production, flags re-validation candidates
(Optional) Custom faion-ba-validator-agent — single-purpose Sonnet agent that emits the quality scorecard JSON; cheaper than running Opus on a checklist task
gherkin-lint — Lint Gherkin AC for ambiguity / style; cheap pre-validation pass. npm i -g gherkin-lint
vale — Prose linter with custom styles ("avoid weasel words", "no TBD"). Drives the lexical half of the unambiguity check. vale.sh
proselint — English clarity/consistency linter; complements vale on structural prose. pip install proselint
markdownlint-cli2 — Catches malformed tables in spec.md (broken traceability matrices). npm i -g markdownlint-cli2
lychee — Verifies all spec/design hyperlinks resolve — broken trace = broken validation. cargo install lychee
git-blame-someone-else (or git log -p spec.md) — Show who last touched each requirement; informs re-validation scope. builtin git
pandoc — Convert spec.md to PDF/DOCX for non-engineer reviewers; required for many regulated sign-offs. pandoc.org
mermaid-cli — Render trace matrices / decision flows from spec.md for the validation packet. npm i -g @mermaid-js/mermaid-cli
Reqnroll / Cucumber — Execute AC as living tests so validation outcomes survive into CI. reqnroll.net · cucumber.io
aider / claude (CLI) — Drive the quality scorecard prompt against spec.md from a pre-commit hook. aider.chat · docs.anthropic.com
Jira + Xray / Zephyr (SaaS) — Requirements ↔ test ↔ validation result trace; agents POST issue logs to the requirement ticket.
Azure DevOps (SaaS) — Native "Acceptance Criteria" + "Reviewers" fields; sign-off captured as work-item state transition.
Linear (SaaS) — Lightweight; AC + issue log live in description; convention-driven validation tags.
Polarion / Jama Connect (SaaS) — Strong reqs lifecycle + formal sign-off, regulated industries (medical/aero). Heavy for solo.
ReqView (Desktop + cloud) — ReqIF import/export; agents can transform but UX is human-driven.
DocuSign / Adobe Sign (SaaS) — Auditable sign-off layer over a generated sign-off PDF (pandoc).
Loom / tl;dv / Otter.ai (SaaS) — Record + transcribe validation sessions; transcript → minutes agent.
GitHub Issues + sub-issues (SaaS) — Issue log as task list; validation closes via PR merge. Works for OSS/solo.
Confluence / Notion (SaaS) — Pre-read packets, draft minutes; weak on traceability — pair with a real reqs tool.
Storybook + Chromatic (SaaS/OSS) — Prototype-review validation: visual diff against approved baseline; agents can flag drift.
Playwright test.step (OSS) — Simulation-style validation: replay user task scripts, capture deviations.
Validate small, validate often. Cap any one session at ~25 requirements; the anti-pattern of "200 in one go" is the single most common failure mode.
Right reviewers, named. A session without the actual decision-maker is preparation, not validation. Agent-prepared agendas must list specific names + roles, not "Product Owner TBD".
Severity discipline. Every issue gets H/M/L plus a root cause class (missing info, ambiguous wording, conflict, infeasible, gold-plating). Drives re-validation scope.
Two-pass sign-off. Pass 1 reviews the requirements; pass 2 reviews the resolved issue log against pass 1. Single-pass sign-off masks unresolved items.
Traceability matrix is non-optional. REQ → source → AC → test ID → review status, in spec.md or a generated artefact. Agents enforce this with the script above.
Prototype + simulation beat documents for UI/process reqs. When validating UX or process flows, switch technique — Storybook click-through or BPMN-driven walk beats reading a numbered list.
Capture conditions, not just approvals. "Approved subject to X" without X tracked is the same as "rejected, but politely". Conditions go on a tracked owner with a resolution status.
Re-validate on substantive change. Heuristic: any edit that changes acceptance behaviour, NFRs, or external interfaces re-triggers validation; copy-edits do not.
Living docs. Generate the validated requirements snapshot into the SDD done/ folder (markdown + signed PDF if regulated); this becomes the auditable baseline.

Hallucinated approvals. Models generate "Approved by: Product Owner" when no human approved. Mitigation: require a verifiable evidence anchor (chat permalink, ticket transition, signed PDF hash) before any resolved/approved state.

Confirmation-bias scoring. When asked "are these requirements OK?", LLMs trend toward yes. Use adversarial framing: "find at least 3 ways each requirement could be misinterpreted" before asking it to grade.
Lost source provenance. Agents quote spec.md back to itself instead of the elicitation source. Force evidence_quote to come from the source document, not the artefact under review.
Severity inflation. Agents tag every issue High to look thorough. Anchor severity to a concrete rule: H = blocks design, M = needs clarification before implementation, L = wording.
Scope drift via "while we're here". Agents propose new requirements during validation. Validation modifies, it does not extend — extensions return to elicitation.
Stakeholder simulation. Tempting to skip humans by role-playing "as Product Owner". Disallowed for sign-off. Acceptable only as a dry run feeding the agenda.
Token-cost trap on large specs. Loading the full spec.md per attribute per requirement explodes cost. Batch by section; cache the design.md / source elicitation packet.
Locale and accessibility blindness. Agents default to English/USD/desktop happy paths and silently pass i18n/a11y reqs. Add a per-validation locale + a11y matrix prompt slot.
Human-in-the-loop checkpoints. Three are mandatory: (1) reviewer selection, (2) decision capture during the session, (3) sign-off. Agents prepare and transcribe; humans decide.

Dont conflate validation with verification. Agents will quietly run tests and call it validation. The methodology is explicit: validation = right thing; verification = built right. Keep the artefacts and gates separate.

template.purpose: Per-requirement quality checklist covering all eight attributes plus completeness and issue log
Validation session agenda template with time slots, participant roles, and pre-work list
Requirements sign-off form with scope, conditions, outstanding items, and signature table
